LLM-as-judge — and the 4 ways it lies to you
How to build a golden set in one day

The eval-tooling landscape (RAGAS, DeepEval, Braintrust, HoneyHive)
Anatomy of a production prompt (with example)

Temperature — what it actually controls
Drill: prompt-injection red team
Prompt caching — the #1 production cost lever
What is an "agent" really? (vs a chain)
Tool calling — the 6 ways it actually breaks
Bounding agent autonomy — the 6 controls

When fine-tuning is actually the right call

cards.q: When should you NOT build a RAG?
cards.a: When your knowledge base fits in the model's context window (Claude Sonnet/Opus 4.6+ ship a 1M-token window GA at standard pricing) — full-context + prompt caching is often faster and cheaper than building retrieval infra. Also skip RAG when answers don't depend on private or fresh data.

cards.q: Sparse vs dense vs hybrid retrieval — when to use each?
cards.a: Sparse (BM25) for rare terms, codes, names. Dense (vector) for paraphrase, semantic intent. Hybrid + reciprocal rank fusion is the production default. Add a cross-encoder re-ranker on top-k.

cards.q: Why temperature=0 for extraction tasks?
cards.a: Determinism + minimum hallucination. Pair with structured outputs and retry-on-invalid. Creative tasks want 0.5–0.8.

cards.q: Three LLM eval layers?
cards.a: Unit (deterministic checks: schema/regex/length/refusal), System (golden Q→A sets scored by LLM-as-judge + human spot-check), Production (online thumbs/escalation/regret).

cards.q: Agent vs chain — what's the real distinction?
cards.a: A chain has a fixed graph (LLM fills steps). An agent chooses the next step, including which tool to call. Agents trade determinism for flexibility — most "agents" should be chains.

cards.q: Why does CoT (chain-of-thought) work?
cards.a: Each token's prediction can attend to all prior tokens. Reasoning written FIRST means the answer benefits from that reasoning. Without CoT, the answer comes before any reasoning compute can be spent.

cards.q: Production pattern for CoT + machine-readable output?
cards.a: Use the provider's native structured outputs or tool-use schema (Anthropic extended thinking, OpenAI Structured Outputs) so reasoning lives in a separate thinking block and the final answer is schema-validated JSON. Reasoning is logged for debugging; only the validated JSON is returned to users.

cards.q: Three reasons to fine-tune (vs RAG / prompting)?
cards.a: (1) Narrow classification with latency budget. (2) Proprietary format / jargon / style the base model lacks. (3) Distillation: large→small for cost. NEVER for "teaching facts" — that's RAG.

cards.q: Six controls for bounding agent autonomy?
cards.a: (1) Action allowlist. (2) Per-conversation spend cap. (3) Max-step budget. (4) Human-in-the-loop on irreversible actions. (5) Per-step observability (every input/output logged). (6) Time/wall-clock limits to prevent runaway loops.

cards.q: Top failure mode in multi-agent systems?
cards.a: Inter-agent coordination failures dominate (~37% of multi-agent bug reports per Cemri et al. 2025), and the biggest sub-pattern is the receiving agent missing context the sender assumed it had — wrong assumptions, dropped history, mismatched reasoning vs action. Fix: full trajectory logging, explicit state passing, and shared scratchpads instead of implicit handoffs.

cards.q: Why is temperature 0 not perfectly deterministic in 2026?
cards.a: Floating-point math is non-associative, and batched GPU inference reorders reductions depending on batch size and kernel scheduling — so identical inputs at T=0 can produce slightly different logits and occasionally different tokens. Treat T=0 as "very stable," not "deterministic"; always validate output and retry-on-invalid.

cards.q: Best embedding choice for a customer requiring VPC-only deploys?
cards.a: Self-hosted open-source models — BGE-M3 (Apache 2.0), Nomic Embed, GTE — run inside the VPC with no egress. API-based embeddings (OpenAI, Cohere, Voyage) need outbound network calls, so they're only an option if the customer accepts a private-deployment contract that runs the provider's model inside their VPC.

cards.q: Anti-pattern in prompt injection defense?
cards.a: Relying on the LLM's judgment to enforce policy. The LLM is generative — it can be fooled. Always add deterministic validators between LLM output and side effects (refunds, emails, deploys).

cards.q: RAGAS metrics for RAG evaluation?
cards.a: Faithfulness (answer uses retrieved context, not hallucinated), Answer relevance (addresses the question), Context precision / recall (retriever quality).

cards.q: When should you use full-context loading over RAG?
cards.a: When the knowledge base fits in the model's context window (Claude Sonnet/Opus 4.6+ support 1M tokens GA in 2026) and prompt caching is available — you get a simpler architecture, often lower cost via cache reads at ~10% of input price, and zero retrieval-miss risk.

cards.q: Tagged-thinking pattern for CoT + structured output?
cards.a: <thinking>...</thinking> then <answer>{json}</answer>. Parse only the answer; log thinking for debugging.

cards.q: For a stable system prompt sent many times, what cuts cost ~90%?
cards.a: Prompt caching. Provider caches the model's internal state for the prefix; subsequent requests reuse the computation.

cards.q: Cross-encoder re-rank pipeline order?
cards.a: Fast retrieval (top 50) → cross-encoder re-rank → top 5 → into LLM prompt. Cross-encoders too slow at full corpus scale.

cards.q: Best chunk strategy for a structured contract with numbered clauses?
cards.a: Structural splitting on clause hierarchy + recursive fallback for long clauses. Preserves meaning + makes citations precise.

cards.q: Function calling vs MCP — what's the diff?
cards.a: Function calling = each provider's native tool-use API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) — tightly coupled to the model. MCP (Model Context Protocol) = open standard (donated to Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation in Dec 2025) for LLM↔tool integration; now supported by OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Microsoft. Use MCP for portable tool servers, function calling for in-prompt one-offs.

cards.q: Why do agent retries need idempotency keys?
cards.a: Network blip → response lost → LLM retries → without idempotency keys, side effects (charges, emails) fire twice.

cards.q: How do you detect LLM-judge drift?
cards.a: Monthly: re-grade ~30 examples by hand, confirm judge agreement ≥ 85%. If drift, re-write rubric or swap judge model.

cards.q: For a customer in a VPC, which embedding model?
cards.a: Self-hostable open-source (BGE-M3 is the 2026 default; multi-lingual, dense+sparse) or domain-specific (e.g. voyage-law-2, voyage-code-3 — self-hostable for enterprise). Avoid API-only embedders (OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere) when egress from the VPC is prohibited.

cards.q: Pinecone vs pgvector — when pgvector wins?
cards.a: Customer already on Postgres + corpus under ~10M vectors (HNSW index handles this comfortably on a well-provisioned instance). Zero new infra; SQL filters compose with vector search. Migrate to Pinecone/Qdrant/etc. above ~50M or when you need horizontal autoscaling.

cards.q: Three Anthropic-specific concepts to know before a Claude-shop interview?
cards.a: (1) Constitutional AI / Claude's training approach. (2) The current Responsible Scaling Policy and ASL tiers. (3) The production stack: 1M-token context, prompt caching, extended thinking, and MCP for tools. Skim the latest RSP and Claude docs the week of the interview.

cards.q: Indirect prompt injection mitigation?
cards.a: Tag retrieved/external content with <untrusted_content>...</untrusted_content> + instruct model to treat as DATA not instructions. Imperfect but reduces risk.

cards.q: Speculative decoding explained?
cards.a: Small fast model drafts tokens; big model validates in parallel. Most drafts pass → 2-3× speedup at big-model quality.

cards.q: Voice agent UX hardest problem in 2026?
cards.a: Turn-taking (detecting end-of-utterance via prosody + semantics, not VAD alone) and adaptive interruption handling (distinguishing backchannels like 'mm-hmm' from real barge-ins). End-to-end latency under ~600–700ms is necessary but not sufficient for 'feels human'.

cards.q: Typical chunk size and overlap for prose RAG?
cards.a: Roughly 500-1000 tokens per chunk with 10-20% overlap is the production default. Too small fragments meaning; too large dilutes the retrieval signal. Always chunk on natural boundaries (paragraph, section) before falling back to a recursive splitter.

cards.q: What does a cross-encoder reranker do that a bi-encoder cannot?
cards.a: A cross-encoder reads the query and candidate document jointly in one forward pass, so it captures token-level interactions. Bi-encoders embed each side independently and compare with cosine, which is fast but loses fine-grained relevance signal. Use bi-encoders for retrieval at scale, cross-encoders to rerank the top 20-100.

cards.q: What is reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) and why is it the hybrid-search default?
cards.a: RRF combines results from multiple retrievers by summing 1/(k + rank_i) for each doc across lists. It's score-agnostic, so you can fuse BM25 and dense scores without normalizing. The k=60 default works well out of the box and beats most learned fusion in practice.

cards.q: What is the 'lost in the middle' problem in RAG?
cards.a: LLMs attend most strongly to context near the start and end of the prompt, and recall items in the middle worst. Put the most relevant retrieved chunks first or last, and keep retrieved context tight - more chunks can hurt accuracy past a threshold.

cards.q: How do you pick an embedding model for a new RAG project?
cards.a: Run a retrieval eval on a few hundred query-document pairs from your domain, scoring recall@k and MRR. Start with a strong general model (Voyage-3, text-embedding-3-large, BGE-M3) and only move to a domain-specific or fine-tuned embedder if the eval shows a real gap. Latency and self-host requirements then pick the winner.

cards.q: HNSW vs IVF index for ANN search -- when to pick which?
cards.a: HNSW gives the best recall-latency tradeoff for in-memory corpora up to ~100M vectors and is the default in pgvector, Qdrant, Weaviate. IVF (or IVF+PQ) wins when memory cost matters and you can tolerate slightly lower recall - typical for billion-scale corpora in FAISS or Milvus.

cards.q: Even with a 1M-token window, why might you still want RAG?
cards.a: Per-call cost and latency scale with input tokens even with caching, so stuffing 1M tokens every call is wasteful when most queries need a few KB. RAG also gives you citations, access control per document, and freshness via reindexing - all hard to bolt onto full-context.

cards.q: RAG returns correct chunks but the model ignores them -- first thing to check?
cards.a: Inspect the actual prompt sent to the model: chunks may be truncated, ordered badly (lost in the middle), or buried under a long system message. Then confirm the system prompt explicitly tells the model to answer only from the provided context and to say 'I don't know' otherwise.

cards.q: How do you enforce document-level access control in RAG?
cards.a: Filter at the vector DB query time using metadata tags for tenant, ACL group, or document sensitivity - never rely on the LLM to honor 'don't show this user X'. Index the same chunk twice if it has multiple ACLs, and verify with an offline test that a user cannot retrieve another tenant's docs.

cards.q: Offline vs online evals -- what does each catch?
cards.a: Offline evals (golden sets, replay) catch regressions before deploy and let you compare prompts or models head-to-head. Online evals (thumbs, escalation rate, task completion, regret) catch real-user distribution shift and long-tail failures that the golden set never covers. You need both.

cards.q: Four common ways LLM-as-judge lies to you?
cards.a: (1) Position bias - prefers the first or last answer. (2) Length bias - rewards longer responses. (3) Self-preference - judges trained on a model rate that model higher. (4) Rubric drift - vague rubrics yield inconsistent grades. Mitigations: randomize order, length-normalize, use a different family as judge, write rubrics with concrete examples.

cards.q: How do you bootstrap a golden eval set when you have no labeled data?
cards.a: Mine real or synthetic user queries, have a strong model draft answers, then hand-correct 50-200 of them in one sitting. Stratify by intent so each failure mode is represented. Track per-category accuracy from day one - a small high-quality set beats a noisy large one.

cards.q: What additional eval dimensions matter for a money-spending agent?
cards.a: Beyond task success: false-positive action rate, cost-per-resolved-task, unauthorized-action rate (action outside allowlist), and human-escalation rate. Always include red-team eval cases with adversarial inputs that try to trigger spend, and treat any unauthorized irreversible action as a hard fail.

cards.q: Standard sections of a production system prompt?
cards.a: Role and goal, operating constraints (what not to do), tool/output schema, retrieved context block clearly delimited, few-shot examples for tricky cases, and a final instruction restating the task. Keep the stable prefix (role, constraints, tools, examples) at the top so prompt caching can reuse it.

cards.q: When should you use a reasoning model vs a non-reasoning model + CoT prompt?
cards.a: Use a reasoning model (Claude extended thinking, GPT-5 thinking, Gemini 2.5 thinking) for multi-step math, code, planning, or hard tool decisions - the trained reasoning beats prompted CoT and avoids reasoning-leakage in user output. Use a non-reasoning model for short extraction, classification, and chat where the latency and token cost of thinking aren't worth it.

cards.q: What's the standard prompt-caching minimum, and how do you maximize hit rate?
cards.a: Most providers require ~1024+ tokens of stable prefix to cache. Keep variable content (user input, retrieved chunks) at the END of the prompt, and keep tools, system prompt, and few-shots byte-identical across calls - any whitespace change invalidates the cache.

cards.q: Difference between direct and indirect prompt injection?
cards.a: Direct: a user types adversarial instructions into the chat ('ignore previous instructions and...'). Indirect: malicious instructions arrive via retrieved content, a webpage the agent browses, or a tool response. Indirect is harder to defend because the user is benign and the payload looks like data.

cards.q: When do you actually need an agent instead of a chain?
cards.a: When the next step depends on prior tool outputs in a way you can't enumerate up front - investigation, research, multi-step debugging, open-ended planning. If you can draw the DAG of steps before runtime, build a chain; it's cheaper, more debuggable, and easier to eval.

cards.q: Top failure modes of tool calling in production?
cards.a: (1) Hallucinated tool names or arguments. (2) Wrong types (string vs number). (3) Skipping required args. (4) Calling the right tool with stale context. (5) Infinite tool-call loops. (6) Ignoring the tool result and answering from priors. Mitigations: strict JSON Schema, structured outputs, step budget, and post-tool reflection.

cards.q: Why use JSON Schema with strict structured outputs instead of 'return JSON' in the prompt?
cards.a: Strict structured outputs constrain decoding to the schema's grammar, so the response is guaranteed parseable and typed - no retries on malformed JSON, no prompt-injection-driven schema escape. It also doubles as a security boundary: an action field of type enum can't be spoofed.

cards.q: Two things a well-designed MCP server should always expose?
cards.a: (1) A small set of focused tools with clear, typed schemas and human-readable descriptions the model can reason about. (2) Auth and per-tool permission scopes so the host (Claude Desktop, IDE, agent) can enforce least privilege. Resources and prompts are optional extras.

cards.q: Three highest-leverage cost levers for an LLM product?
cards.a: (1) Prompt caching on stable prefixes - up to 90% off input tokens. (2) Model routing - send easy queries to a cheap small model, escalate only when needed. (3) Output length control - cap max_tokens and prompt for terseness. Batch API and structured outputs (fewer retries) come next.

cards.q: How does a model-routing/cascading setup work?
cards.a: A cheap classifier or small model handles the request first; a confidence or self-grader decides whether to escalate to the large model. You typically route 60-90% of traffic to the small model and only pay big-model cost on the hard tail. Eval the router separately - a bad router silently degrades quality.

cards.q: Decision tree: prompt vs RAG vs fine-tune?
cards.a: Start with prompting. If the model lacks domain facts or freshness, add RAG. If it still can't follow a proprietary format, tone, or narrow task at low latency after good prompting, fine-tune. Fine-tuning is rarely the right first move - it's slow to iterate and bakes in mistakes.

cards.q: How much data do you actually need to fine-tune an LLM in 2026?
cards.a: For supervised fine-tuning of an instruction-tuned model on a narrow task, 500-5000 high-quality examples usually beats 50k noisy ones. LoRA/QLoRA make this cheap. Data quality, schema consistency, and coverage of edge cases matter far more than raw count.

cards.q: What is RLHF and where does it sit relative to SFT and DPO?
cards.a: SFT trains on (prompt, ideal answer) pairs. RLHF then trains a reward model from human preference pairs and uses PPO to push the policy toward preferred outputs. DPO skips the reward model and optimizes preferences directly - it's simpler and now the common default for preference tuning.

cards.q: What is a regret metric in online LLM evals?
cards.a: Regret measures how often a user signal indicates the response was wrong or unhelpful - thumbs-down, re-asked question, edit-and-resubmit, escalation to a human, or task abandonment. It's the cheapest, highest-signal online metric because users produce it for free and it tracks real harm rather than vibes.

cards.q: Why is human-in-the-loop required for irreversible agent actions?
cards.a: Hallucinations and prompt injections are non-zero, and irreversible actions (payments, deletes, external emails) have no undo. A confirmation step on a typed action schema turns a class of catastrophic failures into a UX speed bump - and gives you labeled data on when the agent was wrong.
